title: Creating a Dementia-Friendly Home
summary: Small, specific changes to a home's layout and lighting can reduce confusion and increase independence for someone with dementia.
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: Creating a Dementia-Friendly Home | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: Small, specific changes to a home's layout and lighting can reduce confusion and increase independence for someone with dementia.
whatYouNeedToKnow: Consistent, simple layouts reduce confusion more than any single gadget.
Labels and visual cues help more than verbal reminders.
Reducing clutter and glare matters as much as any safety device.

paragraphs: Keeping furniture arrangement consistent, using labels with words or pictures on cabinets and doors, and maintaining even, glare-free lighting throughout the day all reduce the cognitive load of simply navigating a space. Clocks and calendars in clear view can help anchor time of day, which is often one of the first things to become confusing.
Reducing visual clutter — patterned rugs, cluttered counters, mirrors that can be mistaken for another person or room — tends to lower agitation more than families expect.

nextSteps: Add clear labels to key cabinets and doors.
Simplify one high-traffic area first, like the kitchen or bathroom.
Take the Home Safety Assessment for a fuller room-by-room review.
disclaimer: General environmental guidance — an occupational therapist experienced in dementia care can offer a more tailored home assessment.

sources.name: Alzheimer's and Dementia Home Safety Tips
